text.content:
Original PDF page 118

IV

epic
MAGIC
Original PDF page 119

Original PDF page 120

The Great Arcanisms

L

egendary Spellcasters and Legendary Foes
alike can access 10th-level spells,
arcanisms tantamount to the most powerful
effects that can occur in your game–
particularly in combat. Only ancient rituals
of great cost or direct divine mandates can
surpass them, and even then, 10th-level
spells remain the building blocks of those realitybending workings. They are just about the hardest thing
a mortal can learn.

Epic Magic

The scale of 10th-level spells is beyond that of any nonLegendary effect. As noted in the Magic section of the
introduction, Epic Magic encompasses any effect that
surpasses 9th level–divine authority, Wish-like
workings, and, of course, 10th-level spells. Epic Magic is
the only thing that can withstand or alter Epic Magic.

Many effects you will encounter in the following
pages are singular, well-defined 10th-level arcanisms. In
your Legendary Campaign, however, you may come
across a variety of Epic Magic effects that are broader or
less certain. A demigod’s gift of invulnerability, a divine
dragon’s unique breath weapon, a void creature’s ability
to fracture reality with each strike–even the powerful
effects of a Relic-level magical item! The DM
determines what qualifies as Epic Magic, and therefore
what can only be interacted with, countered, or replaced
by an equally powerful Epic effect. Non-Legendary
magic–even effects as sweeping as Antimagic Field–
cannot stop Epic Magic.

Clashes of Epic Magic
What happens when two divine heralds clash in their
proclamations? What happens when you cast counterspell
at 10th level to stop an enemy from casting a 10th-level
spell? In these situations, the DM has considerable latitude
in making a ruling, but we suggest erring on the players’
side and using contests and rolls to settle complex
interactions. Shutting down your player’s Miracle with a
10th-level counterspell is brutally anticlimactic. You can ask
yourself the following questions to reach a sound ruling
between “it just works”, “it doesn’t work”, and “it may work,
with a roll”.

Which caster is mightier or more skilled in magic?
+

+

Is the caster of the challenged effect present to try and
overcome the challenge, or is the magical effect
standing on its own?

What would allowing a challenge to this effect imply in
worldbuilding terms?
+

+

And, perhaps most important of all, how would your
ruling affect the fun of the table? Both you and your
players are here for a good time, not realism.

The Cost of Epic Magic

Casting a 10th-level spell requires an action, a 10thlevel spell slot, and 2 Legendary Points. This is an
extraordinary investment of resources in the middle of
combat. When executed properly, Epic Magic can have
such an effect as to halt a battle in its tracks.

These great arcanisms become available at 22nd
level, and spellcasters learn at least one additional 10thlevel spell before their Legendary Adventure is over. A
great part of a spellcaster’s power budget is baked into
these effects.

The 10th-Level Spell Slot

The 10th-level spell slot introduced in this expansion
also allows existing spells to be upcast to a higher level.
Upcasting with a 10th-level slot, however, is a
categorically different matter from a standard upcast.
To use a 10th-level spell slot this way, you must still
expend 2 Legendary Points. A spell upcast at 10th level
gains additional features–less powerful than those of a
true 10th-level spell, but greater than a standard upcast.
This is a great way of creating your own 10th-level
spells!

When a spell is upcast using a 10th-level spell slot, it
can gain one or more of the following enhancements, at
the DM’s discretion:

+

Greater Scale. The spell’s area of effect, range, or
number of targets increases significantly beyond its
standard upcast progression. A 10th-level Meteor
Swarm may bring down enough comets to raze a
city, rather than just a small district.

+

Heightened Potency. The spell’s damage, healing,
or primary effect increases beyond its standard
upcast progression. A 10th-level Greater Restoration
may end all the harmful effects listed in its
description, rather than just one of them.

+

Lingering Effect. The spell leaves a persistent
consequence in its wake–a zone of altered terrain, a
lasting condition, or a secondary effect that
continues long after the spell ends. A 10th-level
Time Stop may leave a small zone with permanent
disruptions in the flow of time, such as a loop or
accelerated/slowed time.

+

Unendurable. The spell ignores resistances and
treats immunities as resistances. A 10th-level
Fireball may harm even fire elementals, so arcanely
suffused the spell becomes.

+

Unavoidable. Creatures that succeed on their
saving throw against the spell still suffer a reduced
version of its effect, rather than none at all. A 10thlevel Disintegrate may still deal half damage to a
creature that succeeded on the save.

Original PDF page 121

Material Components

10th-level spells require material components of
incredible value. Unless those components are
consumed upon use, they are mostly flavorful additions
to the mechanics of the game; as the DM, it is up to you
whether these are required or a focus can do the trick.

Magic in your Game

D&D 5e / 5.5e has some difficulties with certain highlevel spells. In a Legendary Campaign, where 9th-level
spells are plentiful and often go unused between long
rests, a character might choose to cast Wish, dubbed
the “mightiest spell a mortal can cast”, every night until
they are unable to do so anymore. This can quickly
unravel your game if you aren’t prepared.

There are some fixes that can be proposed for certain
spells, though a Legendary Campaign can very well
contend with these effects as written. Legendary Foes
have the tools not to be overwhelmed by unusual spell
combinations accumulated over a long campaign.

That said, the following spells deserve special
attention at your table. Each entry describes why the
spell may become problematic at Legendary levels, and
offers a fix you can adopt. As the DM, feel free to take as
many or as few as you’d like into your table, and
communicate clearly with your players about how these
effects have changed. As a player, be patient with your
DM! They are trying to make this fun for everyone
without non-legendary legacy rules getting in the way of
the narrative.

Wish. Is it really the mightiest spell a mortal can cast, if
there are 10th-level spells in the roster? That aside, with
the legendary level progression, it becomes realistic for
a caster to use it two or even three times a day,
depending on their class. Hardly the epic, one-of-a-kind
arcanism it was in non-legendary play…

Our Fix: 9th-level Wish is now called Lesser Wish
and has greater limitations, allowing only to create the
stated effects, or similarly powerful ones–not being
considered Epic Magic. 10th-level Wish, called True
Wish, expands on those effects and is actually the
mightiest spell a mortal can cast. More about these
changes on the True Wish spell!

Simulacrum. At Legendary levels, a duplicate of a
22nd-level spellcaster is a second legendary character
at the table, quickly breaking the intended balance of
legendary encounters.

Our Fix: A Simulacrum created at Legendary levels
remains at 20th-level and doesn’t have access to
Legendary Points, resistances, and other legendary
features.

Forcecage and other elements of force. An
inescapable cage that blocks teleportation is already
one of the most frustrating spells in the game at the 7thlevel category. Against a Legendary Foe with no reliable
saving throw bonus against Dexterity, it can trivialize
encounters entirely.

Our Fix: Legendary Foes and Legendary Characters
treat Forcecage as a contested effect they can break out
of using their strongest ability score. When they end
their turn trapped in a construction of magical force,
they make a DC 30 ability saving throw of their choice.
On a success, they break out of their confinement in a
way consistent with their chosen ability: a Barbarian
might break through the forcefield, tearing it apart with
their bare hands, while a Wizard may find a weakness
in the arcane matrix of the wall, destabilizing it with a
precisely calculated burst of prana. The construction of
magical force closes behind the character after their
escape. This can also be used in other cases of magical
confinement of a Legendary Creature, as long as that
magical confinement comes from a non-Legendary
effect, a Legendary Foe or Character will always be able
to escape it. Additionally, we recommend letting
mythical techniques and Artifact and Relic weapons
destroy constructions of magical force.

Shapechange and True Polymorph. The ability to
become any creature with a Challenge Rating equal to
your level is already extraordinarily broad at 17th level.
At 22nd, it can give access to stat blocks that dwarf
what any encounter was designed to handle.

Our Fix: Shapechange and True Polymorph have a
CR limit of 20.

Teleport, Plane Shift, and Gate. Escape spells are a
natural part of 5e, and in most campaigns, a reasonable
one. In a Legendary Campaign where the DM has
invested in complex, multi-session scenarios, a single
Teleport can bypass entire narrative arcs.

Our Fix: Create more zones that don’t allow for
teleporting in or out of them with a non-Epic effect!
Maintain the ability to teleport within the boss arena, for
example, but close the planar boundaries around it,
ensuring there is no chance of running away for either
your players or your boss monster, or at least not
without significant resource expenditure.

Magical Rulings
As a DM, you will probably make a few questionable rulings,
especially when it comes to how certain magical effects
interact. That’s okay. Don’t be afraid of changing things up
if they are clearly not working as intended. Use your gut!
Perhaps you find that some spell is having too much impact
during a battle; don’t let that spell trivialize an encounter
because of a technicality.

Also, keep in mind the Tiers of Magic rule from the
introduction, which allows you to determine that a certain
magical effect or feature does not come into play when
measured against greater magic; perhaps you determine
that Death Ward (4th-level), powerful as it is, is completely
bypassed by the 10th-level spell Killing Winds, even if that is
not specifically written out. It’s your call!

Original PDF page 122

Epic Spells

The following is a list of all Epic Spells available to
Legendary Characters within this supplement. Epic
Spells belong to the eight established schools of magic
and are segmented by class. Take the time to evaluate
all your options whenever you gain access to a 10thlevel spell, and create a plausible flavor-like explanation
for your learning of a certain arcanism. This is your
ultimate power-up–how did you come across it?

LP Cost. As a Legendary Character, casting a 10thlevel spell requires 2 Legendary Points, an action, and
the appropriate spell slot. Legendary Foes play by
different rules – with multiple actions and recharging
LP, casting a 10th-level spell is often a multi-round
endeavor and a costly matter, giving the party a chance
to react before the great arcanisms are unleashed upon
them.

Epic Spells

Name
School
Classes

Abjure
Abjuration
Sorcerer, Wizard

Allmage
Divination
Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard

Apocalypse
Evocation
Cleric, Druid, Warlock, Wizard

Avatar
Transmutation
Cleric, Druid, Sorcerer, Warlock

Cloak of Barbatos
Necromancy
Cleric, Sorcerer, Warlock

Divine Rift
Conjuration
Cleric, Wizard

Greater Simulacrum
Illusion
Wizard

Horde to Stone
Enchantment
Bard, Sorcerer, Warlock

Killing Winds
Necromancy
Cleric, Warlock

Mass Foresight
Divination
Bard, Druid, Warlock, Wizard

Miracle
Evocation
Cleric, Druid

Patron’s Blast
Evocation
Warlock

Rimley’s Pulse
Abjuration
Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard

Theophrastus’ Flux
Transmutation
Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard

True Wish
Conjuration
Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard

Wrath of Nature
Conjuration
Druid

Epic Magic System

Many 10th-level spells are very useful in combat, but
much like in regular D&D, some are part of the broader
flavor of the Epic Magic system rather than tools your
players will reach for in a fight. Apocalypse, for
example, will rarely see use on the players’ side unless
they are actively trying to devastate a region or unmake
a plane of existence. That does not mean the spell need
not be written! Not only can it serve as the signature
power of a villain, it can also appear within the pages of
an ancient grimoire or as the source of a catastrophe
your players are racing to prevent.

When evaluating an Epic Spell, consider not just what it
does in a fight, but what story it tells and what role it
might play in the hands of your world’s most powerful
figures–among which, the players!

Original PDF page 123

Abjure

10th-level abjuration

Classes: Sorcerer, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 reaction, which you take when a
creature you can see within 120 feet of you casts a spell
Range: 120 feet
Components: V, S, M (a platinum symbol of the
Abjuration school worth at least 1.000 GP)
Duration: Instantaneous

With a powerful word of abjuration, you interrupt the
casting of a spell. If the creature is casting a spell of 9thlevel or lower, it fails and has no effect. The creature
must then make a Charisma saving throw. On a
success, it cannot cast spells of the same school of
magic as the interrupted spell for the next 24 hours. On
a failed save, the target’s magical conduits decompose,
causing them to take 10d10 force damage and
becoming unable to cast spells. The target’s conduits
can be restored by a casting of Regenerate or similar
magic.

If you use this spell against a creature that is casting a
10th-level spell, you must make a spellcasting ability
contest against them. If you succeed, they suffer the
spell’s effects as normal. If you fail, Abjure has no effect.

Allmage

10th-level divination

Classes: Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: Self
Components: V, S, M (a silvery alphabet containing all
42 magical runes that make up Toth’s cypher of
Sorcery)
Duration: Concentration, up to 1 minute

Your eyes turn to ever-splitting silvery mirrors as you
divine the core runes that make up the arcane world.
Choose a spellcasting list.

You gain the ability to cast any spell of 9th level or lower
from that list, as if you knew them and had them
prepared. While you maintain concentration on this
spell, they count as spells of your class for you.
Additionally, for the duration of this spell, when you cast
a spell of 8th level or lower from the chosen spell list,
you do not expend a spell slot.

Original PDF page 124

Apocalypse

10th-level evocation

Classes: Cleric, Druid, Warlock, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 hour
Range: 10 miles
Components: V, S, M (a handful of earth from the
body of Geb, a living flame from the plane of fire, a gust
of wind from Zephyr’s courtyard, and a gallon of silver
water from the fountains of Atlantis)
Duration: Concentration, up to 8 hours

You open a rift to the four elemental planes,
commanding the elements to create an apocalyptic
natural disaster capable of laying waste to a city. When
you finish casting this spell, the elements surround you,
and a 10-foot radius beam of energy descends from the
heavens to illuminate you for the duration. The beam
sheds bright light out to 60 feet, and dim light for
another 60 feet. For the duration of the spell, you
levitate 60 feet off the ground and must use your action
every turn to channel the elements. If you move or are
moved outside of the energy beam, or you lose
concentration, the spell ends. This spell can only be cast
under an open sky. At the start of each of your turns for
the duration of this spell, choose one of the following
effects to take place in a location of your choosing
within range.

Earth. A terrible earthquake ravages the land. Choose a
point within range. A powerful seismic disruption
occurs in a 200-foot-radius circle centered on that point.
Hundreds of fissures open in the area, swallowing
whatever is on the surface, leaving only a crater in the
earth. Each creature within the area must make a
Dexterity saving throw, taking 10d6 bludgeoning
damage on a failure and falling prone into the crater, or
half as much on a success and not falling prone. A
creature that can fly automatically succeeds this saving
throw. The ground in the area becomes difficult terrain.
Each creature on the ground that is concentrating must
make a Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the
creature’s concentration is broken.

The tremor deals 50 bludgeoning damage to any
structure in contact with the ground in the area when
you use this effect, and each time you choose this effect
again until the spell ends. If a structure drops to 0 hit
points, it collapses and potentially damages nearby
creatures. A creature within half the distance of a
structure’s height must make a Dexterity saving throw.
On a failed save, the creature takes 5d6 bludgeoning
damage, is knocked prone, and is buried in the rubble,
requiring a DC 20 Strength (Athletics) check as an
action to escape. The GM can adjust the DC according
to the nature of the rubble. On a successful save, the
creature takes half as much damage and doesn’t fall
prone or become buried.

Air. You create four violent tornadoes that assail the
area. Choose four points you can see within range.

Tornadoes appear in 15-foot radius, 120-feet tall
cylinders centered on each point. The tornadoes move
up to 30 feet at the start of each of your turns in a
random direction and last for one minute. You can use
this effect again to choose the direction of the
tornadoes, or to create four tornadoes in new locations,
replacing the previous four. Any creature that starts its
turn within a tornado must make a Strength saving
throw. On a failed save, the creature takes 5d6
bludgeoning damage and is pushed 20 feet in a random
direction and 30 feet up. On a successful save, the
creature takes half as much damage and isn’t pushed.
Flying creatures make this saving throw with
disadvantage. Objects that weigh up to 200 pounds are
also pushed around by the tornado. A creature that is
pushed in this manner lands prone and takes 6d6
bludgeoning damage.

Water. Torrential rain assails a 400-foot-radius circle
centered on a point you can see within range, drowning
the area in a matter of minutes. The area becomes
lightly obscured. All creatures within it must succeed on
a Strength saving throw or be swept up by the water
and moved 60 feet in a direction determined by the flow
of the current. A creature that fails this saving throw by
5 or more is pulled under the water and starts drowning
if it breathes air.

Fire. You summon a pillar of radiance and flame in a
30-foot radius, 200-feet-tall cylinder centered on a point
within range. Each creature within the area must make
a Dexterity saving throw, taking 8d8 fire damage and
8d8 radiant damage on a failed save, or half as much on
a successful one. The fire spreads around corners. It
ignites flammable objects in the area that aren’t being
worn or carried. Objects ignited by the flames of this
spell burn at twice the normal rate and require twice as
much water to put out.

Original PDF page 125

Avatar

10th-level transmutation

Classes: Cleric, Druid, Sorcerer, Warlock
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: Self
Components: V, S, M (a diamond worth at least

10,000 G.P, which the spell consumes)
Duration: 1 hour

You transform into an Avatar, a gargantuan elemental of
arcane energy. You can have your avatar take any form
you like, from a titanic animal to a spiritual version of
yourself. You must have the space to grow to
Gargantuan size without squeezing.

When you cast this spell, you enter a trance-like state
and experience the world through the Avatar. You are

Avatar
Gargantuan elemental (titan), any alignment

Armor Class 25 (natural armor)

Hit Points 350 (20d20 + 140)

Speed 60 ft., fly 30 ft. (hover)

STR
DEX
CON
INT
WIS
CHA

30 (+10)
8 (-1)
25 (+7)
X (-)
X (-)
X (-)

Damage Immunities poison; slashing, piercing and
bludgeoning from nonmagical attacks
Condition Immunities charmed, frightened, poisoned,
exhaustion
Saving Throws Strength +17, Constitution +14
Senses truesight 120 ft., passive Perception equal to that of
the caster
Languages –
Challenge 23 (50,000 XP)

Proficiency Bonus +7

Limited Magic Immunity. The Avatar can’t be affected or
detected by spells of 6th level or lower unless it wishes to be.
It has advantage on saving throws against other spells and
magical effects. These benefits extend to the caster.

Avatar. The Avatar has the same Intelligence, Wisdom, and
Charisma scores as its caster.

entombed in its center, unable to move, and are
provided with full cover from all attacks and effects
originating outside the Avatar. Lingering effects, such as
a poison or curse, still affect your regular form while
you are inside the Avatar. For the duration of the spell,
you control the Avatar, whose statistics are outlined in
the next page. While entombed within the Avatar, you
cannot cast spells, speak or perform any other actions
that its form cannot accommodate. When the spell ends,
you float slowly to the ground. The spell ends early if the
Avatar is reduced to 0 hit points, you are reduced to 0
hit points, or your body is transported outside of the
Avatar. Your body remains visible inside the Avatar, and
can be targeted by effects that aren’t rendered moot by
full cover. The Avatar provides you with benefits to
saving throws and protections against certain effects, as
detailed in its stat sheet.

Magic Weapons. The Avatar’s weapon attacks are magical.

Siege Monster. The Avatar deals double damage to objects
and structures.

Immutable Form. The Avatar is immune to any spell or effect
that would alter its form.

Actions

Multiattack. The Avatar makes two Slam attacks and uses its
Beam.

Slam. Melee Weapon Attack: +17 to hit, reach 30 ft., one
target. Hit: 24 (4d6 + 10) bludgeoning damage.

Beam. The Avatar shoots a beam of pure energy forming a
line 100 feet long and 5 feet wide in a direction of its choice.
Each creature in the line must make a DC 22 Dexterity saving
throw. A creature takes 35 (10d6) force damage on a failed
save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

Primordial Endurance. The Avatar prepares for an incoming
strike. Until the end of its next turn, it gains resistance to all
damage except psychic damage.

Arcane Meteor (1/day). The Avatar hurls an arcane meteor
onto a point within 500 feet of it. Each creature in a 40-footradius sphere centered on that point must make a DC 22
Dexterity saving throw. A creature takes 49 (15d6) force
damage and 49 (15d6) bludgeoning damage on a failed
save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

Original PDF page 126

Cloak of Barbatos

10th-level necromancy

Classes: Cleric, Sorcerer, Warlock
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: self (15-foot radius, 5-foot-high cylinder)
Components: V, S, M (a cigar of infernal tobacco
worth at least 100 GP, which the spell consumes)
Duration: Concentration, up to 1 minute

You exhale deathly smoke from your nose and mouth,
which coalesces into a huge cloak of decay that wraps
around you, killing everything in your path. The cloak is
a 15-foot-radius, 5-foot-high cylinder that moves with
you. Creatures that enter that area for the first time in a
round of combat must make a Constitution saving
throw, taking 8d6 x 10 necrotic damage on a failed save,
or half as much on a successful one. A creature that is
reduced to 0 hit points by this spell is liquified, dying
instantly and having its body destroyed.

Divine Rift

10th-level conjuration

Classes: Cleric, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: 120 feet
Components: V, S, M (a rapier-sized ornamental
needle worth at least 10.000 GP)
Duration: Until dispelled

You speak a word of power, and the continuum of space
is rearranged to create a passage between your location
and another location anywhere in the cosmos. A rift,
one inch thick and up to 20 feet tall and 20 feet long,
appears centered in an unoccupied space of your choice
within range, and another of the same size appears at a
specific spot of your choice on the destination. This
spell can connect two points between different planes of
existence, even to dimensions unknown to you. If you
are unfamiliar with the destination, the portal appears
in an advantageous position near your intended point of
arrival, though not exactly on target. If you attempt to
connect your base with the castle of an evil overlord in
another plane of existence, for example, the Divine Rift
might appear outside the evil overlord’s stronghold,
behind the cover of a forest wall.

If you are familiar with the destination point, the
Divine Rift forms exactly where you choose.

Only creatures of your choice can use the rift. You can
also designate a passcode that can be spoken to open or
close the passage through it. Creatures that walk
through the rift appear on the other side as if the two
locations were contiguous.

You can only have one Divine Rift active at a time. If
you cast this spell again, any previous Divine Rifts you
have opened are dispelled. You can choose to close the
Divine Rift at any point as an action.

This spell overcomes all restrictions for interplanar
shifts, allowing travel anywhere within the universe,
regardless of divine mandate. It can access unknown
dimensions, from ancient demiplanes to heavily
guarded tombs of old. A deity or creature of similar
power is still capable of closing the rift by focusing their
divine power, but can only do so after one minute has
passed since the spell was cast.

Original PDF page 127

Greater Simulacrum

10th-level illusion

Classes: Wizard
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: 120 feet
Components: V, S, M (a shadow caught in a bottle, a
handful of snow from Tartarus, a shard of glass from
the City of Mirrors, and a pinch of salt sprinkled in the
air)
Duration: Concentration, up to 1 hour

You shape an illusory duplicate of up to four creatures
within range. The duplicates are shadowy forms,
partially real, and can take actions and be affected as a
normal creature would. The illusions use all the
statistics of the creatures they duplicate, except that
they are Constructs and can have a maximum of 300 hit
points. A duplicate forms with all of the target’s
equipment, except for Artifacts and Relics.

These duplicates do not possess the ability to cast
10th-level spells nor use features or abilities that
expend more than 2 Legendary Points at a time. They
otherwise have all the abilities of their real
counterparts, mimicking their targets’ capabilities with
uncanny precision. A simulacrum cannot create another
simulacrum nor recover Legendary Points.

The simulacri are friendly to you and creatures you
designate. They obey your mental commands (no action
required), moving and acting in accordance with your
wishes. The duplicates act after their real counterparts
in the initiative order. The simulacri lack the ability to
learn or become more powerful. They cannot learn new
spells, gain class levels, or recover spent resources.

The simulacri last for an hour, until they are reduced
to 0 hit points, or until your concentration ends, at
which point they de-materialize into fading wisps of
shadow. When a simulacrum is destroyed, any lingering
effects it has created disappear alongside it.

Original PDF page 128

Horde to Stone

10th-level enchantment

Classes: Bard, Sorcerer, Warlock
Casting Time: 1 minute
Range: 10 miles
Components: V, S, M (a blank puppet and a steel pin)
Duration: concentration, up to 1 hour

Describe the targets you wish this spell to affect with as
much or as little detail as you choose. You can specify
creature types, age ranges, genders, allegiance to a
sovereign or organization, alignment, and similar
criteria. The description must be short enough to be
spoken within this spell’s casting time. As you speak,
the puppet used in the casting magically acquires the
features of the creatures you describe. To complete the
casting, you must drive the steel pin into the heart of the
puppet.

When you finish casting this spell, a wave of arcane
energy washes out from the puppet, and each creature
that fits your description within range must succeed on
a Wisdom saving throw or be petrified. If you
concentrate for the full duration of the spell, the
petrification lasts until dispelled.

Killing Winds

10th-level necromancy

Classes: Cleric, Warlock
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: 500 feet
Components: V, S, M (the carcass of an infernal locust
and the favor of a deity)
Duration: Instantaneous

A powerful wind lifts from a point you choose within
range, and with it, the last breath of all mortals.
Creatures with less than 100 hit points in a 100-footradius sphere centered on that point die instantly,
dropping limp where they stand. Creatures with more
than 100 hit points must succeed on a Constitution
saving throw or die instantly. The wind violently swings
open doors and windows and extinguishes open flames,
but affects no other physical entities. Creatures
surrounded by at least 10 feet of solid matter are not
affected by this spell.

Mass Foresight

10th-level divination

Classes: Bard, Druid, Warlock, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: 120 feet
Components: V, S, M (a divination fractal with six
surfaces worth at least 1,000 gp, which the spell
consumes)
Duration: 8 hours

You tap into the possibilities contained within the
fractal, splitting it into up to six shards, which you can
bestow upon willing creatures within range. Each shard
affixes to a creature’s forehead as a tiny item with an AC
equal to the bearer’s AC + 5, 30 hit points, and
immunity to all damage except force. A fractal shard can
be destroyed or dislodged from a creature’s forehead by
a creature grappling the bearer as an action.

A creature bearing a fractal shard gains a limited ability
to see into the immediate future. It can’t be surprised
and has advantage on attack rolls, ability checks, and
saving throws. Additionally, other creatures have
disadvantage on attack rolls against it.

Original PDF page 129

Miracle

10th-level evocation

Classes: Cleric, Druid
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: Touch
Components: V, S, M (A shard of moonsilver worth
1,000 G.P)
Duration: Instantaneous

You place your hands upon a creature you can see
within range, summoning a surge of positive energy that
floods its body and spirit. The target regains all its hit
points, it is cured of all conditions affecting it, including
those that would normally require a specific spell or
ability to remove, and it is freed from any curse or
harmful magical effect affecting it.

This spell restores all of the target’s expended class
features, abilities, and spell slots. Only spell slots of 9th
and 10th-level, uses of Mythical Techniques, and
legendary class features with limited usage are not
restored.

Alternatively, you can target the body of a creature
that has been dead for no longer than one minute. This
spell restores them to life with all their hit points and
regrows missing body parts, if any. This use requires
only a diminutive part of a deceased creature–a hair, a
tooth, its ashes, or disintegrated remains suffice to
recreate the rest of the body.

Miracle can end, cure, or dispel effects that would
otherwise require a Wish spell, such as reconstructing a
soul that was destroyed. The DM has final say on what
effects are within the scope of Miracle to cure.

Patron’s Blast

10th-level evocation

Classes: Warlock
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: Self (240-foot line)
Components: V
Duration: Instantaneous

You speak a word of power and release the full,
unrestrained might of your patron in a catastrophic
burst of disintegrating energy. A 240-foot long, 10-foot
wide line of raw magical force erupts from you, its color
and shape a reflection of your patron’s nature–a wall of
hellfire, a celestial streak of silver light, an eldritch tide
of writhing darkness.

Each creature in the line must make a Constitution
saving throw, taking 8d10 x 10 force damage on a failed
save, or half as much on a successful one. A creature
that fails this saving throw by 5 or more is also
incapacitated until the end of its next turn, as the blast
tears through it. If this damage reduces a creature to 0
hit points, it is disintegrated. A disintegrated creature,
along with everything it wears and carries except magic
items, is reduced to a pile of fine gray dust. A
disintegrated creature can only be restored to life by a
True Resurrection or Wish spell.

This spell automatically destroys Large or smaller
nonmagical objects and creations of magical force. If
the target is a Huge or larger object or a creation of
force, the spell destroys a 10-foot-cube portion of it.
Rare or rarer magic items are not destroyed.

Original PDF page 130

Rimley’s Pulse

10th-level abjuration

Classes: Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: Self (1 mile radius)
Components: V, S, M (a finely tuned platinum chime
worth at least 10.000 GP)
Duration: Instantaneous

You release a disrupting, high-pitched musical note that
reverberates in a 1-mile radius. An audible hum fills the
area, and every ongoing magical effect of 9th level or
lower is instantly dispelled. Spellcasters in the area lose
concentration on any spell they maintain. Magic items
in the area that aren’t legendary, artifacts, or relics lose
their magical properties and become mundane. A
creature attuned to one or more affected items can
make a single Charisma saving throw; on a success,
those items retain their magic.

Constructs animated by magic that doesn’t originate
from a deity or an artifact must succeed on a
Constitution saving throw or be destroyed.

Theophrastus’ Flux

10th-level transmutation

Classes: Bard, Sorcerer, Warlock, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: Self (15-foot cone)
Components: V, S, M (a transmutation stone worth at
least 5,000 GP)
Duration: Concentration, up to 3 rounds

You manifest a formidable pulse of volatile
transmutation in your hand, nourishing it before setting
it loose in a 15-foot cone in front of you. The pulse
transforms everything in its path into a nonmagical
material of your choice, such as gold, glass, wood, or
sand. Creatures in the area must make a Constitution
saving throw against the spell, taking 8d6 x 10 force
damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful
one. A creature entirely contained within the area of the
spell has disadvantage on the saving throw. If this spell
reduces a creature to 0 hit points, it is completely
transformed into the chosen material. Magic items that
are of Legendary, Artifact, or Relic rarity are not
transformed by this spell.

When you cast this spell, you can hold it to increase its
range and potency, releasing it in one of your
subsequent turns by using an action. To maintain such a
powerful arcanism, you must expend your action on
each of your turns to hold the spell. If you hold it for 1
round, the range becomes a 30-foot cone instead, and
the damage increases to 8d8 x 10. If you hold it for 2
rounds, you can choose to make the range a 60-foot
cone, and the damage increases to 8d12 x 10. If you

hold it for 3 rounds, you can choose to make the range a
90-foot cone, and the damage increases to 8d20 x 10.

If you lose concentration on this spell before you release
it, you regain the Legendary Points you spent on its
channeling, but you lose the 10th-level spell slot.

Original PDF page 131

True Wish

10th-level conjuration

Classes: Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: Self
Components: V
Duration: Instantaneous

Wish, at 10th-level, is the mightiest spell a mortal
creature can cast. By simply speaking aloud, you can
alter the very foundations of reality in accord with your
desires. The basic use of this spell is to duplicate any
other spell of 9th level or lower. You don’t need to meet
any requirements in that spell, including costly
components. The spell simply takes effect.

Alternatively, you can create one of the following effects
of your choice:

+

You create one object of up to 50,000 gp in value
that isn’t a magic item. The object can be no more
than 300 feet in any dimension, and it appears in an
unoccupied space you can see on the ground.

+

You allow up to twenty creatures that you can see to
regain all hit points, and you end all effects on them
described in the Greater Restoration spell.

+

You grant up to ten creatures that you can see
resistance to all damage, including Dark and Lethal
damage, for the next 24 hours.

+

You grant up to ten creatures you can see immunity
to a single spell or other magical effect for 8 hours.
For instance, you could make yourself and all your
companions immune to a lich’s life drain attack.

+

You undo a single recent event by changing the
result of any roll made within the last round
(including your last turn). Reality reshapes itself to
accommodate the new result. For example, a Wish
spell could undo an opponent’s successful save, a
foe’s critical hit, or a friend’s failed save, and replace
it with a new roll.

You might be able to achieve something beyond the
scope of the above examples. State your wish to the GM
as precisely as possible. The GM has great latitude in
ruling what occurs in such an instance; the greater the
wish, the greater the likelihood that something goes
wrong. This spell might simply fail, the effect you desire
might only be partly achieved, or you might suffer some
unforeseen consequence as a result of how you worded
the wish. For example, wishing that a villain were dead
might propel you forward in time to a period when that
villain is no longer alive, effectively removing you from
the game. Similarly, wishing for a legendary magic item
or artifact might instantly transport you to the presence
of the item’s current owner.

The stress of casting this spell to produce any effect
other than duplicating another spell weakens you. After
enduring that stress, each time you cast a spell until you
finish a long rest, you take 1d10 Soul damage per level
of that spell. This damage can’t be reduced or prevented
in any way. In addition, your Strength drops to 3 if it
isn’t 3 or lower already, for 2d4 days. For each of those
days that you spend resting and doing nothing more
than light activity, your remaining recovery time
decreases by 2 days.

One Wish. True Wish at 10th level is the most
powerful effect you can manifest, as a mortal creature.
Regardless of the effect you bring into existence–even
duplicating a spell or creating one of the effects listed
above–you are unable to cast Wish at 10th level ever
again. Knowing the 10th-level version of Wish still
allows you to cast it at 9th level. Alternatively, at your
DM’s discretion, you can learn a new 10th-level spell
during an appropriate amount of downtime to replace it.

Original PDF page 132

Wrath of Nature

10th-level conjuration

Classes: Druid
Casting Time: 1 action
Range: 1 mile
Components: V, S, M (a bronze twig from the
forgotten forests of Elphame)
Duration: Concentration, up to 8 hours

You call upon the natural world to rise against those
who would threaten it. Choose a point you can see
within range. For the duration, a 500-foot-radius circle
centered on that point becomes a living battlefield, as
every root, stone, and beast awakens with purpose.
Artificial terrain in the area is destroyed and returns to
its original form. Towers of stone become rolling hills,
libraries of wood and paper turn to trees, and dams
release rivers back into the soil. Great structures do not
crumble under the power of this spell, but smaller
nonmagical artificial creations permanently return to
their pre-civilization shapes.

If you cast this spell in a location where nature has
been deliberately corrupted, desecrated, or destroyed –
a razed forest, a poisoned river, a city built upon sacred
land – the spell’s radius doubles and each of its damage
rolls increases by one die. The DM determines whether
a location qualifies.

For the duration, the area is transformed in the
following ways:

+

Difficult Terrain. The ground throughout the area
becomes difficult terrain for any creature you
designate as an enemy when you cast this spell.

+

Ensnaring Growth. At the start of each of your
turns, creatures of your choice within the area must
succeed on a Strength saving throw or become
restrained until the start of your next turn, as roots
and vines surge up to bind them.

+

Beast Tide. A tide of ghostly creatures native to the
terrain – wolves, bears, eagles, serpents, or similar –
surges through the area for the duration. At the start
of each of your turns, each creature of your choice in
the area takes 4d10 Soul damage as the ghostly tide
washes over them. This damage cannot be avoided.

+

Eyes of the Wild. For the duration, you are aware
of the location of every creature within the area, as
the natural world feeds you information through
roots, winds, and tides. You cannot be surprised, and
you gain truesight out to 60 feet.

Boons of Nature. When you cast this spell, you can
designate any number of creatures you can see as
allies. Allied creatures within the area are unaffected
by its hostile effects, ignore difficult terrain, and
regain 2d10 hit points at the start of each of their
turns.

+

Changing Environment. When the spell ends or is
interrupted, the area loses its magical properties but
remains transformed. Trees that have grown over
streets stand tall, rivers that escaped their dams find
new paths, and stones raised from the earth settle into
new positions.

Restoration. This spell can also be cast over a plot of
land in need of healing rather than war. When cast this
way, you can choose to forgo all of the spell’s hostile
effects for the duration, channeling its power entirely
into restoring and revitalizing the land to its natural
state.
